content: ## AI Use in Coursework Students may use AI tools for brainstorming and outlining. Final submissions must reflect the student's own reasoning. Disclosure of AI assistance is required. Here’s an **expanded, student-facing AI Use Policy** that builds directly on your paragraph, keeps the tone supportive (not punitive), and is clear enough to reduce ambiguity and appeals. This is **Canvas-ready**. ------ ## **Acceptable Use of AI in This Class** AI tools (such as ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, or similar systems) are **encouraged** in this course when used thoughtfully and responsibly. Because AI is now part of professional practice, this class treats AI as a **learning aid**, not a shortcut. The guiding principle is simple: > **AI may assist your thinking, but it may not replace your thinking.** ### Permitted Uses of AI Unless an assignment explicitly states otherwise, you may use AI tools for: - **Brainstorming and ideation** (e.g., generating initial ideas, outlining approaches, suggesting alternatives) - **Planning and structuring work** (e.g., organizing arguments, creating outlines, identifying sub-problems) - **Analysis and exploration** (e.g., summarizing perspectives, comparing options, testing assumptions) - **Prototyping and drafting** (e.g., early drafts, sample code, pseudocode, exploratory explanations) These uses are intended to **support learning**, not bypass it. ### Required Disclosure (Always) When you use AI assistance, you must clearly disclose: 1. **Which tool(s)** you used 2. **How** you used them (for what purpose) 3. **What you changed, verified, or added yourself** A short disclosure statement is sufficient. For example: > *“I used ChatGPT to brainstorm possible frameworks for this analysis and to draft an initial outline. I revised all sections, verified factual claims, and developed the final arguments independently.”* ### Your Responsibility as the Author When you submit work in this class, **you are the author**, regardless of AI assistance. That means you are responsible for: - the **accuracy** of all claims - the **logic and reasoning** of the argument - the **originality** of the submission - your ability to **explain and defend** the work If you cannot explain how your submission works or why it is correct, that is a signal of misuse. ### ### Prohibited Uses of AI The following uses are **not permitted** and will be treated as academic misconduct under the Honor Code: - Submitting AI-generated work that you do not understand - Using AI to **misrepresent authorship** (presenting AI output as your own thinking) - Using AI to complete an assignment **explicitly marked as no-AI** - Using AI to evade learning objectives (e.g., bypassing required reasoning, reflection, or explanation) Intent matters, but **outcomes matter more**. If AI replaces your learning, it is being misused. ### Gray Areas and Judgment Not every AI use case fits neatly into “allowed” or “not allowed.” If you are unsure: - **Ask before submitting** - Document your use clearly - Err on the side of transparency Good disclosure will never harm your grade. Undisclosed AI use might. ### Appeals and Review If AI use is questioned: - The instructor will review the work and the disclosure - You may be asked to explain your process or reasoning - You have the right to follow the standard **appeals process** outlined in the syllabus ### **One Sentence to Remember** > **AI can support your work.** > **You remain responsible for it.**

## Appeals Students may appeal within 7 days. The instructor documents evidence and rationale. Unresolved cases escalate to the program director. ## **Appeals and Review** If the use of AI in an assignment is questioned, the goal of the review process is to **understand how the work was produced**, not to assume misconduct. ### **Step 1: Instructor Review** The instructor will first review: - the submitted work - any AI disclosure provided - the assignment requirements and learning objectives At this stage, many questions are resolved through clarification alone. ### **Step 2: Student Explanation** If clarification is needed, you may be asked to: - explain **how** you used AI tools (if any) - describe your **reasoning process** and key decisions - walk through your work (e.g., drafts, notes, code, outlines) This conversation is about **process**, not perfection. ### **Step 3: Determination** After review, the instructor may determine that: - the AI use was **appropriate and properly disclosed** - the AI use was **inappropriate but unintentional**, or - the AI use constitutes **academic misconduct** under the Honor Code You will be informed of the outcome and next steps. ### **Step 4: Right to Appeal** If you disagree with the instructor’s determination, you have the right to appeal. The appeal process follows the **standard procedures outlined in the syllabus** and may include: - submitting a written appeal - providing supporting materials (drafts, notes, version history) - review by a program director, department chair, or designated committee ### **Important Notes** - **Transparency matters.** Clear AI disclosure helps resolve questions quickly. - **Documentation helps.** Drafts, notes, and version history can support your explanation. - **AI detection tools alone are not determinative.** Decisions are based on evidence, context, and explanation. ### **Guiding Principle** **The purpose of review is fairness and learning, not punishment.** Students are encouraged to ask questions early, document their process, and communicate openly if uncertainty arises.

## Academic Integrity Misrepresentation of authorship is a violation. Cases are reviewed by the instructor and may be appealed. ## **Academic Integrity** Academic integrity means that the work you submit **honestly represents your own understanding, effort, and authorship**. This principle applies to all assignments, whether or not AI tools are used. ### **Authorship and Responsibility** You are the author of any work you submit in this course. This means: - the ideas, reasoning, and conclusions reflect your own understanding - you can explain and defend the work you submit - you take responsibility for accuracy, originality, and clarity Using tools (including AI) does not transfer authorship or responsibility. ### **Misrepresentation of Authorship** Misrepresentation of authorship occurs when: - AI-generated content is submitted as if it were your own thinking - AI is used to complete work you do not understand - required AI use is not disclosed - another person’s work or automated output is presented as original Misrepresentation of authorship—whether intentional or not—is a violation of academic integrity. ### **Review and Determination** If concerns arise: - the instructor will review the work, assignment requirements, and any disclosures - you may be asked to explain your process, reasoning, or development of the work - decisions are based on **evidence, context, and explanation**, not automated tools alone AI detection tools may be used as one input, but **they are not determinative by themselves**. ### **Appeals** If a determination is made regarding academic integrity: - you will be informed of the outcome - you have the right to appeal following the procedures outlined in the syllabus - appeals may involve review by a program director, department chair, or designated committee ### **Guiding Principle** **Integrity is about honest learning, not perfect work.** Students are encouraged to ask questions, disclose tool use, and document their process. Transparency and communication protect both learning and fairness.
